title: The art of the midterm transition
gist: Labour peer Andrew Adonis argues the Starmer-to-Burnham handover has no preordained electoral outcome (5 of 10 postwar midterm PM transitions won the next election), that new PMs usually outperform expectations, and that the decisive near-term signal is Burnham's choice of chancellor — with fiscal/Brexit policy currently undefined and market reaction to higher borrowing unaddressed.

themes.theme: Base rates for midterm PM transitions

insights.claim: Of the 10 postwar UK midterm PM transitions, five went on to win the following general election and four lost (Truss being the 49-day outlier), so no preordained outcome attaches to the Burnham handover.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Roughly even base rate means a Burnham bounce is plausible — UK political-risk pricing should not assume Labour is doomed.
insights.quote: Five went on to win the following general election; four went on to lose, while one, Liz Truss, only survived for 49 days.
insights.locator: There have been 10 previous midterm transitions

insights.claim: The exiting PM's degree of unpopularity is a non-factor for the successor's success — Macmillan, Major and Johnson all won after inheriting from collapsed predecessors (Eden, Thatcher, May).
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Starmer's deep unpopularity tells you little about Burnham's electoral odds — discount the 'Labour is finished' narrative.
insights.quote: One definite non-factor is the degree of failure or unpopularity of the exiting prime minister.

insights.claim: New PMs tend to exceed expectations because they aren't blamed for past failures and bring fresh energy, as Brown did via an initial bounce when Labour stopped feuding over Iraq.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Expect Burnham to poll better early; the durable test comes once he must make governing choices.
insights.quote: new prime ministers—apart from the uniquely disastrous Truss—tend to do better than generally predicted when they first enter the role
insights.locator: Insofar as I can detect a trend

themes.theme: Fiscal and Brexit policy uncertainty under Burnham

insights.claim: Burnham looks set to change chancellor in favour of an as-yet-undefined growth policy, with rival Labour camps split between welfare cuts, an undefined wealth tax, and more borrowing.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Policy ambiguity at the top of UK fiscal direction is a gilt-market and sterling watch item until the chancellor is named.
insights.quote: The universal cry is “growth”, but growth is not a policy; it is an objective.

insights.claim: Jim O'Neill's proposal of more borrowing would require completely rewriting the fiscal rules Burnham said last week he would abide by, exposing an unresolved contradiction in his fiscal stance.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Any signalled loosening of UK fiscal rules is a direct gilt-supply and term-premium risk.
insights.quote: would require completely rewriting the fiscal rules which last week Burnham said he would abide by

insights.claim: Adonis suspects Burnham has no view yet on how markets would react to sharply increased borrowing, implying the incoming government is unprepared for a bond-market reaction.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: An unprepared fiscal pivot raises the tail risk of a Truss-style gilt repricing.
insights.quote: nor does he likely have a view on the reaction of the markets if borrowing is sharply increased
insights.locator: Or does he mean he would stick

insights.claim: Burnham agrees Brexit has been an economic disaster and that growth needs a better EU trading deal, but said he won't act on the customs union or single market until the 2029 election.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: No near-term UK-EU trade re-integration catalyst before 2029 — limits the upside case for a Brexit-reversal growth re-rating.
insights.quote: he won’t do anything much about joining the customs union or single market until the 2029 election

insights.claim: The single most clarifying near-term signal is Burnham's chancellor pick, floated between Ed Miliband (left) and Wes Streeting (right) — the choice forces and reveals the government's direction.
insights.speaker: Andrew Adonis
insights.so_what: Watch the chancellor appointment as the cleanest read on UK fiscal-policy direction and market posture.
insights.quote: Until this choice of chancellor is resolved, no one will have much idea about the direction of the next government.
insights.locator: The first requirement is to appoint a chancellor
